headline: ADHD vs Autism vs AuDHD: Key Differences Explained
description: ADHD and autism share many overlapping traits — but AuDHD (having both) is its own distinct experience. Here's how to tell them apart.

name: Lim Min Tze
description: Founder of DoTheThing, a task breakdown tool built for ADHD and AuDHD brains.

itemListElement.name: ADHD vs Autism vs AuDHD: Key Differences Explained

mainEntity.name: Can you have both ADHD and autism?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Research shows that approximately 21% of children with ADHD also meet criteria for autism, and about 28% of autistic children also meet criteria for ADHD. Since the DSM-5 was updated in 2013, the two conditions can be officially co-diagnosed. The informal term for having both is AuDHD.

mainEntity.name: Is AuDHD the same as having both ADHD and autism separately?

acceptedAnswer.text: Clinically, yes — AuDHD refers to the co-occurrence of both conditions. But many people with AuDHD describe the experience as distinct from having ADHD or autism alone. The two neurotypes interact in ways that create their own patterns: internal contradictions like craving routine but being unable to stick to it, or intense social desire paired with social exhaustion.

mainEntity.name: What are the key differences between ADHD and autism?

acceptedAnswer.text: ADHD is primarily a disorder of attention and impulse regulation driven by dopamine dysregulation. Autism is primarily a difference in social communication, sensory processing, and preference for routine driven by different neurological wiring. A key distinction: ADHD brains tend to seek novelty while autistic brains tend to crave sameness — though people with AuDHD experience both drives simultaneously.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if I have ADHD, autism, or both?

acceptedAnswer.text: Formal assessment by a professional trained in both conditions is the only reliable way. Many traits overlap (executive dysfunction, sensory sensitivity, hyperfocus), which makes self-diagnosis difficult. If you strongly relate to traits from both conditions, AuDHD is worth exploring with a clinician who understands the dual presentation.

mainEntity.name: Is AuDHD harder than having just one?

acceptedAnswer.text: Research doesn't directly compare severity, but the AuDHD experience often involves internal conflict — the autistic need for routine vs the ADHD inability to maintain it, or the desire for social connection vs sensory overwhelm in social settings. Many people with AuDHD describe it as a constant negotiation between opposing neurological needs.

mainEntity.name: Can someone with AuDHD mask effectively?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, often very effectively. The ADHD brain's social intuition and pattern recognition can mask autistic social differences, while autistic attention to detail can mask ADHD carelessness. This is why many people with AuDHD are diagnosed later in life — they've developed sophisticated compensatory systems that hide both conditions from others.

mainEntity.name: Why was I diagnosed with both ADHD and autism later in life?

acceptedAnswer.text: Before the DSM-5 was published in 2013, the diagnostic criteria explicitly stated that ADHD and autism could not be co-diagnosed. Clinicians were forced to choose one. The rule was removed when research showed co-occurrence is common. Many adults who were diagnosed with one condition as children actually meet criteria for both.
